title: OWASP — Threat Modeling Cheat Sheet

key_concepts: Four-question framework: What are we working on / What can go wrong / What are we going to do about it / Did we do a good job
STRIDE classification (Spoofing, Tampering, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, Elevation of privilege)
Data flow diagrams (DFDs) and trust boundaries
Decompose-Identify-Mitigate-Validate lifecycle
Continuous threat modeling — refresh on every architectural change
Risk rating with DREAD or simpler likelihood × impact
control_or_capability_taxonomy: Threat enumeration via STRIDE per element
threats_or_risks: Architectural blind spots in new features
Trust boundary violations introduced by integrations
Unmitigated STRIDE categories on critical assets
defensive_controls: Authentication strengthening against spoofing
Integrity controls against tampering (signing, attestation)
Audit logging against repudiation
Least privilege against elevation
richMetadata.summary: OWASP's practical threat-modeling methodology, framed around Shostack's four questions. STRIDE provides the threat taxonomy; DFDs surface trust boundaries; iteration ties threat modeling into the SDLC rather than treating it as a one-time exercise.

content:
OWASP Threat Modeling Cheat Sheet — Methodology and Application

The OWASP Threat Modeling Cheat Sheet codifies the practical methodology for systematically identifying and mitigating threats during software design. It is built around four questions (Shostack's framing): What are we working on? What can go wrong? What are we going to do about it? Did we do a good job?

Methodology — STRIDE per element

The canonical taxonomy is STRIDE: Spoofing identity, Tampering with data, Repudiation, Information disclosure, Denial of service, and Elevation of privilege. STRIDE is applied per element of a data flow diagram (DFD) — for each process, data store, data flow, and external entity, the modeler asks which STRIDE categories apply and what mitigations exist. Trust boundaries on the DFD highlight where data crosses privilege levels and warrant the most rigorous review.

Lifecycle — decompose, identify, mitigate, validate

Threat modeling is not a one-shot artifact. The cheat sheet recommends a continuous loop: decompose the system into its security-relevant elements; identify threats using STRIDE; design mitigations; validate that mitigations are implemented and effective. Threat models are refreshed whenever architectural changes occur — new integrations, new data classifications, new trust boundaries.

Integration with the SDLC

For mature programs, threat modeling fits at the design phase and continues into code review and runtime validation. Findings feed into security requirements, ASVS verification items, and acceptance criteria. The cheat sheet emphasizes that threat models are working documents, not deliverables — their value is in the conversation they drive among engineers, security, and product.

Risk rating

The cheat sheet supports DREAD (Damage, Reproducibility, Exploitability, Affected users, Discoverability) but explicitly notes simpler likelihood × impact scoring is often sufficient. The point is consistency within an organization, not adherence to a specific scale.

Why it matters for CISOs

Threat modeling shifts security left in the SDLC at low tool cost. The framework is methodology-agnostic; it works with Threat Dragon, IriusRisk, Microsoft TMT, or pencil and paper. CISOs evaluating AppSec maturity look for threat modeling as a leading indicator — organizations that threat-model proactively avoid vulnerability classes that organizations relying on penetration testing only discover after deployment.

title: OWASP — Threat Dragon

key_concepts: Open-source threat-modeling tool (web + desktop)
Visual DFD editor with drag-and-drop elements
Built-in STRIDE threat suggestions per element
Mitigation tracking and threat status (open/mitigated/transferred)
JSON-based threat model files (version-controllable)
Integration with GitHub for collaborative review
threats_or_risks: Unmodeled architectures (greenfield projects without threat models)
Threat-model drift (diagrams diverging from deployed reality)
defensive_controls: Threat-model-as-code stored alongside source
Pull-request review gates for threat-model updates
richMetadata.summary: OWASP's open-source threat-modeling tool. Provides a visual DFD editor, STRIDE-based threat suggestions, mitigation tracking, and JSON file format that fits into source control. The practical companion to the Threat Modeling Cheat Sheet.

content:
OWASP Threat Dragon — Open-Source Threat Modeling Tool

Threat Dragon is OWASP's flagship open-source threat-modeling tool. It implements the methodology described in the Threat Modeling Cheat Sheet, providing a visual DFD editor, STRIDE-based threat suggestions per element, and tracking of mitigation status. Available as a web app, desktop app (Electron), and Docker container.

Key capabilities

Threat Dragon supports drag-and-drop DFD construction with processes, data stores, data flows, and external entities. Trust boundaries are first-class elements drawn as dashed regions. For each element, the tool offers STRIDE prompts — spoofing for entities, tampering for data flows and stores, repudiation for processes, etc. The user enumerates threats per prompt, assigns severity, and tracks mitigations through three states: open, mitigated, or transferred.

File format and version control

Threat models are stored as JSON files. This means they can be committed to the source repository alongside the code, reviewed via pull requests, and diffed across architectural changes. Threat-model-as-code is the operating model — the artifact lives in the same workflow as the code it describes.

Integration paths

The GitHub integration allows pulling and pushing threat models directly from repositories, supporting team workflows. The threat library is extensible — organizations can add their own threat catalogs (industry-specific, regulatory, etc.) on top of the default STRIDE library.

Reporting

Threat Dragon generates threat-model reports as PDFs, suitable for compliance evidence (e.g., demonstrating NIST SSDF PW.1 "Design software to meet security requirements and mitigate security risks") and for security review checkpoints.

Why it matters for CISOs

Threat Dragon removes the tool excuse for not threat-modeling. It is free, open-source, easy to deploy, and supports the JSON-as-source-control pattern that engineering teams already follow. The barrier becomes process and discipline, not tooling. CISOs adopting Threat Dragon typically pair it with a threat-modeling policy that requires updated models for any architectural change touching authentication, data classification, or trust boundaries.

title: MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise Matrix (top-level)

key_concepts: Tactics (the why — adversary objectives)
Techniques (the how — adversary behaviors)
Sub-techniques (specific instances of techniques)
Procedures (real-world implementations observed in the wild)
Groups (clusters of activity attributed to threat actors)
Software (malware and tools used by groups)
Mitigations and detections
control_or_capability_taxonomy: 14 Enterprise tactics from Reconnaissance through Impact
Coverage matrices for SOC detection gap analysis
threats_or_risks: Initial Access (T1190 Exploit Public-Facing App, T1078 Valid Accounts)
Execution (T1059 Command-Line Interface, T1106 Native API)
Persistence (T1098 Account Manipulation, T1136 Create Account)
Lateral Movement (T1021 Remote Services, T1550 Use Alternate Authentication)
Exfiltration (T1041 C2 Channel, T1567 Web Service)
Impact (T1486 Data Encrypted for Impact / ransomware, T1485 Data Destruction)
defensive_controls: Detection engineering aligned to specific technique IDs
Behavioral analytics anchored to ATT&CK procedures
Coverage gap analysis via Navigator overlays
richMetadata.summary: Globally adopted knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) observed in real-world intrusions. The lingua franca for detection engineering, purple teaming, and threat-intelligence reporting.

content:
MITRE ATT&CK — The Adversary Behavior Knowledge Base

MITRE ATT&CK is the globally adopted knowledge base of adversary tactics, techniques, and procedures (TTPs) observed in real-world intrusions. It has become the lingua franca for threat intelligence, detection engineering, and red/blue team exercises. The Enterprise matrix covers 14 tactics from Reconnaissance through Impact, organizing approximately 200 techniques and 400 sub-techniques.

Structure — tactics, techniques, procedures

Tactics describe the adversary's objective at a stage of attack (e.g., Initial Access, Persistence, Lateral Movement, Exfiltration). Techniques describe how an objective is achieved (e.g., T1190 Exploit Public-Facing Application for Initial Access). Sub-techniques specify variants (e.g., T1078.004 Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts). Procedures are observed implementations attributed to specific threat groups.

Groups, software, and threat intelligence

ATT&CK catalogs threat actor groups (G0050 APT32, G0007 APT28, G0096 APT41, etc.) with the techniques each is known to use, and the software/malware they deploy. Threat-intelligence reports use ATT&CK technique IDs as the anchor — a Mandiant or CrowdStrike report describing an intrusion will name the techniques observed (T1078, T1021, T1486) so defenders can immediately map detection coverage.

Detection engineering and coverage gap analysis

The defender's primary use of ATT&CK is detection coverage mapping. SOC teams build a coverage matrix showing which techniques they detect, with what fidelity, in which data source. ATT&CK Navigator (a companion tool) lets teams overlay coverage onto the matrix visually, identifying gaps for purple-team exercises and detection-development sprints.

Limitations

ATT&CK is descriptive, not prescriptive. It tells you what adversaries do, not how to defend. The pairing with MITRE D3FEND (defensive countermeasures) closes that gap — D3FEND maps offensive techniques to defensive functions. Programs that adopt ATT&CK without D3FEND or NIST CSF can over-rotate on detection while neglecting prevention and recovery.

Why it matters for CISOs

CISOs use ATT&CK as the foundation for threat-informed defense. Board reporting frames risk in terms of "which adversary groups target our sector and which of their techniques we detect." Detection-engineering team velocity is measured by ATT&CK coverage added per quarter. Threat-intel subscriptions are evaluated on the quality of their ATT&CK mappings — a vendor that delivers technique IDs is faster to operationalize than one that delivers prose.

title: MITRE ATT&CK Navigator

key_concepts: Web-based visualization tool for the ATT&CK matrix
Layer files (JSON) annotating techniques with custom data
Stack multiple layers (e.g., coverage, threat actor TTPs, planned work)
Heatmap-style scoring per technique
Filter by platform (Windows, Linux, macOS, Cloud, Containers, etc.)
Export to Excel and SVG for reporting
control_or_capability_taxonomy: Adversary emulation planning (which techniques to test)
threats_or_risks: Coverage gaps not visualized are easily ignored by leadership
defensive_controls: Quarterly coverage layer with score-and-color heatmap
Per-threat-actor overlay showing relevance to specific adversaries
Detection-engineering backlog as a Navigator layer
richMetadata.summary: Web-based tool that visualizes ATT&CK matrices with custom annotations. The de facto way to communicate detection coverage to executives and to plan purple team exercises against specific adversary playbooks.

content:
MITRE ATT&CK Navigator — Visualizing Detection Coverage

ATT&CK Navigator is the companion visualization tool to the ATT&CK matrix. It allows defenders to annotate techniques with custom data — coverage scores, planned work, threat-actor relevance — and produce heatmap-style visualizations that communicate detection posture to technical and executive audiences.

Layer files as the data model

Navigator's core abstraction is the "layer" — a JSON file that maps technique IDs to scores, colors, and comments. A typical defensive program maintains several layers: a current-coverage layer (every technique scored 0-100 for detection confidence), a per-threat-actor layer (techniques used by APT29 highlighted), a roadmap layer (techniques planned for detection work this quarter), and a purple-team layer (techniques tested in last exercise).

Layers stack. Combining a threat-actor layer with a coverage layer immediately shows which APT29 techniques are detected and which are gaps — turning ATT&CK from a catalog into a directed prioritization tool.

Filtering and platform views

Matrix views can be filtered by platform (Windows, Linux, macOS, Cloud, Containers, Network) so teams focus on environments they actually run. Cloud-native organizations spend most of their time in the Cloud and Containers sub-matrices, while traditional enterprises focus on Windows and the network views.

Use cases

The primary use cases are: communicating coverage to executive leadership (color-coded matrix is more legible than spreadsheets); planning purple-team exercises (which techniques are not yet tested); roadmapping detection work (which gaps to close next); evaluating vendor coverage claims (overlay vendor-claimed techniques on the matrix to spot marketing exaggeration).

Integration with detection content

More mature teams sync Navigator layers with their SIEM rule libraries — every detection rule is tagged with the technique ID it covers, and Navigator pulls counts and confidence scores automatically. This transforms Navigator from a static document into a live coverage dashboard.

Why it matters for CISOs

Navigator is the artifact that gets shown to the board. The score-and-color heatmap makes detection investment legible: red squares are budget asks; green squares are credit-claiming material. CISOs who can produce a credible Navigator coverage layer and explain the gaps in plain language have a substantially easier time defending their detection-engineering budget.

title: MITRE D3FEND — Defensive Countermeasures Knowledge Graph

key_concepts: Knowledge graph of cybersecurity countermeasures
Five top-level defensive tactics: Harden, Detect, Isolate, Deceive, Evict
Countermeasure-to-ATT&CK-technique mappings
Digital Artifact ontology (Files, Processes, Network Traffic, etc.)
Defensive function descriptions tied to real product capabilities
Inferred relationships from technique → digital artifact → countermeasure
control_or_capability_taxonomy: Hardening (e.g., Memory Boundary Tracking, Strong Password Policy)
Detection (e.g., Process Lineage Analysis, File Access Pattern Analysis)
Deception (e.g., Decoy File, Honey User Account)
Eviction (e.g., Authentication Cache Invalidation, Account Locking)
threats_or_risks: Programs that adopt ATT&CK without D3FEND tend to over-rotate on Detect at the expense of Harden and Isolate
defensive_controls: Map each ATT&CK technique to a portfolio of D3FEND countermeasures
Evaluate vendor capabilities against D3FEND function descriptions

NIST: CSF v2 IDENTIFY, PROTECT, DETECT, RESPOND, RECOVER

richMetadata.summary: Counterpart to ATT&CK. Whereas ATT&CK catalogs offensive behaviors, D3FEND catalogs defensive countermeasures and links them to the techniques they mitigate. The map for turning detection coverage analysis into a defense roadmap.

content:
MITRE D3FEND — The Defensive Counterpart to ATT&CK

D3FEND ("Detection, Denial, and Disruption Framework Empowering Network Defense") is MITRE's knowledge graph of cybersecurity countermeasures. Whereas ATT&CK catalogs what adversaries do, D3FEND catalogs what defenders do and explicitly links the two. It is the missing piece that turns ATT&CK from "here's what's happening to you" into "here's what to do about it."

Five defensive tactics

D3FEND organizes countermeasures into five top-level tactics: Harden (reduce attack surface), Detect (observe and analyze adversary activity), Isolate (limit blast radius and lateral movement), Deceive (mislead the adversary), and Evict (remove adversary access). Each tactic decomposes into specific countermeasures — e.g., under Detect, you find Process Lineage Analysis, File Access Pattern Analysis, Network Traffic Analysis, Authentication Event Correlation.

Digital Artifact ontology

The key innovation is the Digital Artifact ontology — a structured vocabulary of the things countermeasures observe and manipulate (Files, Processes, Network Traffic, Authentication Events, etc.). ATT&CK techniques produce artifacts (a malicious process execution produces a Process artifact); D3FEND countermeasures consume artifacts (Process Lineage Analysis operates on Process artifacts). This artifact-mediated link is what enables technique-to-countermeasure mapping.

Mapping ATT&CK to D3FEND

For any ATT&CK technique, D3FEND provides a portfolio of mapped countermeasures. T1059.001 PowerShell execution, for example, maps to Process Lineage Analysis (Detect), Script Execution Analysis (Detect), Authorization Database Access Pattern Analysis (Detect), and Code Signing (Harden). Defenders use these mappings to ensure that every high-risk technique has multi-layer countermeasure coverage rather than relying on a single detection.

Program implications — the Harden/Isolate gap

A pattern D3FEND surfaces: programs that adopt ATT&CK in isolation tend to over-invest in Detect and under-invest in Harden and Isolate. ATT&CK's emphasis on technique observation biases toward SIEM and EDR spend. D3FEND counter-balances by showing that Harden countermeasures (memory safety, strong authentication, code signing) prevent many techniques from succeeding at all, and Isolate countermeasures (network segmentation, process sandboxing) limit blast radius when prevention fails.

Vendor evaluation

D3FEND function descriptions are precise enough to evaluate vendor capabilities. A vendor that claims "behavioral detection" can be pressed: do you do Process Lineage Analysis, File Access Pattern Analysis, or just signature-based matching? The framework gives defenders a vocabulary for separating marketing from capability.

Why it matters for CISOs

D3FEND is the bridge between threat-informed defense and capability roadmapping. CISOs who use ATT&CK + D3FEND together can articulate a defensive program as a portfolio of countermeasures with explicit coverage of the techniques their threat model includes. This is more legible to boards than "we have EDR" and more defensible to auditors than "we follow NIST."

title: NIST SP 800-218 — Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) v1.1

key_concepts: Four practice groups: Prepare the Organization (PO), Protect the Software (PS), Produce Well-Secured Software (PW), Respond to Vulnerabilities (RV)
42 outcome-focused practices applicable across SDLC methodologies
Mapping to OMB M-22-18 federal software-supply-chain requirements
Self-attestation for federal software vendors
Tasks expressed at outcome level — implementation is methodology-agnostic
control_or_capability_taxonomy: PO — define security requirements, implement roles and responsibilities, implement supporting toolchains
PS — protect all forms of code from tampering, maintain provenance for software
PW — design software to meet security requirements and mitigate security risks, review and analyze software for vulnerabilities
RV — identify and confirm vulnerabilities on an ongoing basis, assess, prioritize, and remediate vulnerabilities
threats_or_risks: Supply chain compromise via dependency tampering
Unsigned build artifacts
Inadequate vulnerability disclosure handling
defensive_controls: SBOM generation and signature
Reproducible builds
Code review and SAST/DAST gates
Coordinated vulnerability disclosure policy
richMetadata.summary: NIST's secure software development framework, mandated for federal software vendors under OMB M-22-18. Defines 42 outcome-focused practices across four groups (Prepare, Protect, Produce, Respond). The U.S. federal anchor for secure SDLC.

content:
NIST SP 800-218 — Secure Software Development Framework (SSDF) v1.1

SSDF is NIST's secure software development framework, mandated for federal software vendors under OMB M-22-18 (the post-SolarWinds executive order). It defines 42 outcome-focused practices organized into four groups, applicable to any SDLC methodology — waterfall, agile, DevOps, or hybrid.

The four practice groups

Prepare the Organization (PO) covers foundational program elements: defining security requirements (PO.1), establishing roles and responsibilities (PO.2), implementing supporting toolchains (PO.3), and protecting the software development environment itself (PO.5). These are organization-level controls — the program must exist before product-level practices can be operationalized.

Protect the Software (PS) addresses tamper-resistance of code and artifacts: PS.1 "Protect all forms of code from unauthorized access and tampering" includes source code access control, signed commits, and protected branches. PS.2 "Provide a mechanism for verifying software release integrity" requires signed artifacts and reproducible builds. PS.3 covers provenance — knowing where every component of the release came from.

Produce Well-Secured Software (PW) is the design-and-build phase: PW.1 design to meet security requirements (threat modeling), PW.2 review designs (security architecture review), PW.4 reuse existing well-secured software (vetted libraries and components), PW.7 review and analyze human-readable code (code review and SAST), PW.8 test executable code (DAST, fuzzing, penetration testing).

Respond to Vulnerabilities (RV) covers post-release: RV.1 identify and confirm vulnerabilities on an ongoing basis (vulnerability disclosure program, monitoring CVEs in dependencies), RV.2 assess, prioritize, and remediate (SLAs for fix windows by severity), RV.3 analyze root causes (post-incident review feeding back into PW practices).

OMB M-22-18 attestation

Federal software vendors must attest to SSDF compliance via the CISA Secure Software Self-Attestation Form. The form requires the CEO or designee to attest that the organization follows the SSDF practices, with specific reference to artifacts demonstrating each practice. SBOM submission is a related but separate requirement.

Industry adoption beyond federal

Large enterprises now require SSDF attestation from vendors as part of procurement, even outside federal contracts. JPMorgan Chase, Walmart, and several Fortune 100 buyers cite SSDF in vendor risk questionnaires. The practical effect is that any vendor selling to large enterprises needs SSDF readiness even if they don't sell to the federal government.

Why it matters for CISOs

SSDF is the U.S. federal anchor for secure SDLC. Compliance maps cleanly to procurement requirements, and the practice structure aligns with what mature engineering organizations already do (code review, SAST/DAST, dependency scanning, signed builds). For CISOs, SSDF provides the vocabulary for conversations with engineering leadership about secure-by-design responsibilities — and the leverage to get them prioritized.

title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF) 1.0

key_concepts: Four core functions: Govern, Map, Measure, Manage
Trustworthiness characteristics: valid, reliable, safe, secure, resilient, accountable, transparent, explainable, privacy-enhanced, fair
Risk = adverse impact × likelihood, evaluated across the AI lifecycle
Generative AI Profile addresses LLM-specific risks (NIST AI 600-1)
Not prescriptive — provides a structure for organizational risk programs
control_or_capability_taxonomy: GOVERN — policies, accountability, risk tolerance, supply-chain governance
MAP — context, AI system categorization, impact assessment
MEASURE — analytic, qualitative, quantitative assessment of AI risks
threats_or_risks: Confabulation / hallucination
Harmful bias / homogenization
Data poisoning
Information integrity (deepfakes, generated misinformation)
Information security (prompt injection, model extraction)
Intellectual property (training-data IP claims)
Obscene content / CSAM
Privacy (training-data leakage, inference attacks)
Value chain and component integration (third-party LLM use)
defensive_controls: AI risk register tied to MAP outputs
Pre-deployment red-teaming and adversarial testing
Content provenance (watermarking, C2PA)
Human oversight and override mechanisms
Continuous monitoring of model drift and degradation

ATTACK: Cloud and SaaS adversary techniques applied to AI agents

richMetadata.summary: NIST's framework for managing risks to individuals, organizations, and society from AI systems. Four functions (Govern/Map/Measure/Manage) plus ten trustworthiness characteristics. The Generative AI Profile (AI 600-1) extends to LLM-specific risks.

content:
NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF) 1.0

The AI RMF is NIST's framework for managing risks to individuals, organizations, and society from AI systems. Published in January 2023 and extended with the Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1) in July 2024, it has become the U.S. policy anchor for AI risk governance — referenced in the EU AI Act, executive orders, and most enterprise AI governance programs.

Four core functions

The framework organizes AI risk management around four functions: GOVERN (policies, accountability structures, risk tolerance, supply-chain considerations for AI vendors), MAP (system context, categorization, impact assessment — what this AI system does and what could go wrong), MEASURE (analytic methods to assess risks — quantitative metrics where possible, qualitative assessments where not), and MANAGE (prioritization, response, monitoring, communication of residual risk).

Trustworthiness characteristics

The framework names ten characteristics of trustworthy AI: valid and reliable, safe, secure and resilient, accountable and transparent, explainable and interpretable, privacy-enhanced, and fair (with harmful bias managed). These characteristics are evaluated across the AI lifecycle from design through deployment and retirement. The framework does not prescribe acceptance thresholds — organizations define their own risk tolerance.

Generative AI Profile (AI 600-1)

The GenAI Profile enumerates LLM-specific risks: confabulation (hallucination), harmful bias and homogenization, data poisoning, information integrity (deepfakes), information security (prompt injection, model extraction), intellectual property concerns, obscene content, privacy (training-data leakage, inference attacks), and value-chain risks (using third-party foundation models). For each risk, the profile suggests mitigations across GOVERN, MAP, MEASURE, MANAGE.

Integration with security frameworks

The AI RMF complements rather than replaces traditional cybersecurity frameworks. NIST CSF v2 still governs the security of AI systems as IT assets. The AI RMF adds AI-specific risks (confabulation, bias, model extraction) that don't fit cleanly into the CSF threat model. Practical programs map AI risks to CSF functions where appropriate (e.g., model extraction is a DETECT/PROTECT problem) and use AI RMF for the residual.

Enterprise operating model

Most enterprise AI governance programs implement the AI RMF as: a cross-functional AI risk council (GOVERN); a pre-deployment risk assessment template for new AI use cases (MAP); ongoing red-teaming and bias evaluation (MEASURE); and a register of AI systems with owner-accountability and quarterly reviews (MANAGE). The framework supplies the structure; the content is organization-specific.

Why it matters for CISOs

The CISO is increasingly the de-facto owner of AI governance — partly because AI risks are emerging, partly because no other function has the cross-cutting authority. The AI RMF gives CISOs an external anchor when arguing for AI governance investment: "NIST defines this as a four-function discipline; we currently do parts of two." The Generative AI Profile is especially valuable for evaluating LLM vendor contracts, since the risk taxonomy maps to specific contractual protections to demand.

title: OWASP — Top 10 for Large Language Model Applications, 2025

key_concepts: Top 10 risks specific to LLM-integrated applications
LLM01 Prompt Injection (direct and indirect)
LLM02 Insecure Output Handling
LLM03 Training Data Poisoning
LLM04 Model Denial of Service
LLM05 Supply Chain Vulnerabilities
LLM06 Sensitive Information Disclosure
LLM07 Insecure Plugin Design / Excessive Agency
LLM08 Excessive Agency
LLM09 Overreliance
LLM10 Model Theft
control_or_capability_taxonomy: Output validation against expected schema and policy
Vendor due diligence for foundation models and embeddings providers
PII and secrets redaction at prompt and output boundaries
threats_or_risks: Direct prompt injection (user inputs malicious instructions)
Indirect prompt injection (LLM ingests poisoned external content)
Excessive agency (LLM granted tool/action permissions exceeding need)
PII leakage via training data memorization or prompt context
Hallucination consumed downstream as fact (overreliance)
defensive_controls: Treat all LLM output as untrusted; validate schema before action
Principle of least privilege for LLM tool-use scopes
Out-of-band confirmation for high-impact agent actions
Content filtering on inputs from external sources (web, email, documents)
Audit logging of all prompts and LLM-initiated actions

ATTACK: T1059 Command and Scripting Interpreter (when LLM executes commands)

richMetadata.summary: Practical risk taxonomy for LLM-integrated applications. The first widely adopted vocabulary for LLM-specific vulnerabilities — prompt injection, excessive agency, training-data leakage. Tracks alongside ASVS and the broader OWASP catalog.

content:
OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications — The Practical Risk Taxonomy

The OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications has emerged as the de facto risk taxonomy for LLM-integrated software. Updated in 2025 with explicit treatment of agentic AI (tool use, autonomous actions), it provides the vocabulary that AppSec teams, AI engineers, and procurement now use to discuss LLM-specific risks.

The ten risks (2025 ordering)

LLM01 Prompt Injection — direct (user inputs malicious instructions that override system intent) and indirect (LLM ingests poisoned content from web, email, documents, retrieval sources). Indirect prompt injection is the more dangerous variant because the user is often a victim, not an adversary.

LLM02 Insecure Output Handling — treating LLM output as trusted and passing it directly to downstream systems (SQL execution, command execution, HTML rendering) enables classic injection attacks via the LLM as a relay.

LLM03 Training Data Poisoning — adversaries influence model behavior by contaminating training or fine-tuning data; matters most for self-trained or fine-tuned models on user-contributed content.

LLM04 Model Denial of Service — token-budget exhaustion, recursive tool invocation, or compute-amplification attacks that drive up cost or degrade availability.

LLM05 Supply Chain Vulnerabilities — risks from foundation models, embeddings APIs, vector databases, and the broader LLM toolchain.

LLM06 Sensitive Information Disclosure — LLM emits PII or secrets from training data, prompt context (leakage across user sessions), or system prompts.

LLM07 Insecure Plugin / Tool Design — plugins and tool integrations with weak authentication, overbroad permissions, or no input validation.

LLM08 Excessive Agency — the LLM has been granted tool-use permissions or autonomy exceeding the trust earned by its reliability. The flagship 2025 risk as agentic deployments scale.

LLM09 Overreliance — humans treating LLM output as fact and not validating; downstream consumers acting on hallucinations.

LLM10 Model Theft — exfiltration of proprietary model weights via query-based extraction or compromise of model-hosting infrastructure.

Agentic AI extensions

The 2025 revision expanded Excessive Agency into a flagship concern. The principle: the autonomy granted to an LLM agent should be calibrated to the LLM's demonstrated reliability and to the impact of its potential errors. Granting an LLM unrestricted access to email, calendars, or financial systems is the AI-era equivalent of granting any application full domain-admin rights — and the failure modes are far less predictable.

Mitigation patterns

The consensus mitigations: treat all LLM output as untrusted (output validation, schema enforcement, sandboxed execution); apply least privilege to tool-use scopes; require out-of-band human confirmation for high-impact actions; filter inputs that the LLM will ingest from external sources; log every prompt and action for audit and incident review.

Why it matters for CISOs

LLM Top 10 is the AppSec extension every security program needs as LLM features ship across the enterprise. It supplies the language for code review of LLM integrations, the risk register categories for AI use cases, and the procurement questions for LLM vendor evaluation. CISOs whose AppSec teams don't yet know these ten risks are operating blind to a vulnerability class that's now widespread.

title: CISA — Secure by Design

key_concepts: Three principles: take ownership of customer security outcomes; embrace radical transparency; build organizational structure and leadership to achieve these goals
Secure by Default — products should be secure out of the box without expert configuration
Memory safety roadmap — eliminating C/C++ memory-unsafe languages from new code
MFA-by-default at no extra cost
Default-on logging at no extra cost
CVE attribution to the vendor whose product had the defect, not just the deployer
Secure by Design Pledge — public commitments by software vendors
control_or_capability_taxonomy: Eliminate entire classes of vulnerability (memory safety, SQL injection)
Reduce attack surface in default configurations
Make multi-factor authentication free and on by default
Provide logging at no additional cost
Publish vulnerability disclosure policies and authorized researcher safe harbor
threats_or_risks: Customers expected to be security experts to safely deploy products
Vendors shipping known-defective software classes (memory safety bugs in C/C++)
Security features paywalled as premium add-ons (especially MFA, logging)
defensive_controls: Procurement clauses requiring Secure by Design Pledge signatories
RFP scoring criteria that prefer pledge-signing vendors
Vendor risk assessment with Secure by Design checklist
richMetadata.summary: CISA's initiative to shift security responsibility upstream from customer to vendor. Anchored on three principles (take ownership, radical transparency, accountable leadership) with concrete product expectations: no default passwords, free MFA, default-on logging, memory-safe languages for new code.

content:
CISA Secure by Design — Shifting Responsibility Upstream

CISA's Secure by Design initiative, launched in 2023 under then-director Jen Easterly, reframes the cybersecurity problem as a software-quality problem and the responsibility as upstream of the customer. The thesis: customers should not be expected to be security experts to safely deploy software products. Vendors must ship products that are secure by default and secure by design.

Three principles

The framework rests on three principles: Take Ownership of customer security outcomes (rather than disclaiming via warranty language and customer-misuse blame); Embrace Radical Transparency (publish vulnerability disclosure policies, post-incident root cause analyses, and roadmaps to retire known-bad practices); Build Organizational Structure and Leadership to make these commitments durable (CEO-level accountability, not bolted-on security team).

Concrete product expectations

The initiative names specific product changes: eliminate default passwords; eliminate entire classes of vulnerability (memory safety violations, SQL injection); reduce attack surface in default configurations; make multi-factor authentication free and on by default; provide logging at no additional cost (logs are how customers detect compromise — paywalling them is hostile); publish vulnerability disclosure policies with safe harbor for researchers.

Memory safety roadmap

CISA has explicitly called out memory-unsafe languages (C, C++) as a class of defect that should not appear in new code. The October 2024 update encourages vendors to publish memory-safety roadmaps: how new code will use memory-safe languages (Rust, Go, modern Java/.NET) and how existing C/C++ code will be progressively rewritten or sandboxed. Big vendors (Microsoft, Google) have published their roadmaps. Mid-tier vendors are increasingly being pressed to do the same.

Secure by Design Pledge

The Pledge is a public commitment by software vendors to seven specific goals (MFA, default passwords, vulnerability classes, security patches, vulnerability disclosure, CVEs, evidence of intrusions). As of 2025, over 240 vendors have signed including most major SaaS, EDR, and identity vendors. The Pledge is becoming a procurement signal — enterprise buyers and federal agencies are using signatory status as an RFP scoring criterion.

Integration with NIST SSDF and CMMC

Secure by Design complements NIST SSDF (which is the practice-level detail) and CMMC (which is the assessment regime for federal contractors). CISA provides the customer-facing vocabulary and the shaming mechanism; NIST provides the engineering practices; CMMC provides the verification.

Why it matters for CISOs

Secure by Design gives CISOs leverage in vendor negotiations. "Are you a Secure by Design Pledge signatory?" is a procurement question with growing weight. "Why are you charging extra for MFA?" is a Pledge-anchored complaint. For CISOs, the framework moves arguments about vendor responsibility from individual deals into the broader market discourse — softening the ground for procurement and risk teams to push back.

title: CSA — Cloud Controls Matrix v4.1

richMetadata.summary: CSA's cloud security control framework. 207 control objectives across 17 domains, with cross-mappings to NIST, ISO, PCI, HIPAA. CAIQ is the vendor self-assessment companion; STAR Registry publishes CSP submissions for procurement use.

content:
CSA Cloud Controls Matrix (CCM) v4.1 — Cloud Security Control Catalog

The Cloud Security Alliance's Cloud Controls Matrix is the dominant control catalog for cloud security. Version 4.1 contains 207 control objectives organized across 17 domains, with explicit mappings to NIST CSF, NIST SP 800-53, ISO 27001/27017/27018, PCI DSS, HIPAA, and other industry frameworks. It is paired with CAIQ — the Consensus Assessments Initiative Questionnaire — which is the vendor self-assessment companion.

The 17 domains

The domains cover the full cloud security operating model: Audit Assurance & Compliance, Application & Interface Security, Business Continuity Management, Change Control & Configuration Management, Cryptography Encryption & Key Management, Data Center Security, Data Security & Privacy, Governance Risk & Compliance, Human Resources, Identity & Access Management, Interoperability & Portability, Infrastructure & Virtualization Security, Logging & Monitoring, Security Incident Management, Supply Chain Management, Threat & Vulnerability Management, Universal Endpoint Management.

Shared responsibility embedded

A distinguishing feature of CCM is the embedded shared responsibility model. Each control objective is annotated for the parties responsible: Cloud Service Provider (CSP), Customer, or Shared. This codifies what is otherwise an informal conversation in every cloud adoption — exactly where the provider's responsibility ends and the customer's begins for each control.

CAIQ and the STAR Registry

CAIQ is the questionnaire form of CCM — for each control objective, the CSP answers "Yes/No/NA" with explanatory detail. CSPs publish their CAIQ in the public STAR Registry (Security Trust Assurance and Risk). The registry has become a procurement filter: an enterprise security team evaluating a new SaaS vendor will start by checking STAR for a current CAIQ before sending custom questionnaires.

Three levels of STAR

STAR offers three assurance levels. Level 1 is self-assessment (publish your CAIQ). Level 2 is third-party audit (a recognized auditor verifies CAIQ responses against evidence). Level 3 is continuous auditing (real-time monitoring of control effectiveness). Most CSPs are at Level 1 or Level 2; Level 3 remains rare due to operational complexity.

Integration with other frameworks

The cross-mapping is the practical superpower. A customer compliant with PCI DSS can identify which CCM controls already cover PCI requirements and which need additional work. A SaaS vendor demonstrating CCM compliance simultaneously demonstrates partial compliance with ISO 27001, NIST 800-53, and others — reducing duplicate audit work.

Why it matters for CISOs

CCM is the practical baseline for cloud vendor risk assessment. CISOs use it to: structure SaaS vendor due diligence (CAIQ-driven); set internal cloud-security control standards (CCM is the catalog they map to); benchmark cloud posture against peers (STAR provides comparable submissions). For multi-cloud organizations, CCM provides one vocabulary across AWS, Azure, GCP, and Oracle — avoiding the trap of separate per-cloud security frameworks.
